title: Classical Illusion
definition: The idea that the classical world of separate objects is a useful illusion, with our perception constantly collapsing quantum potentiality into stable reality.

difficulty_levels.beginner: Just as you can change the state of tiles by focusing your attention on them, so too does the act of observation in quantum physics cause particles to collapse from a wave-like blur into definite positions.
difficulty_levels.intermediate: The observer effect in quantum physics demonstrates that the act of measurement or observation collapses the wave function of a particle, causing it to take on a specific state. This implies that our everyday perception is a constant act of collapsing reality into a stable, classical world.
difficulty_levels.expert: Our brains evolved to filter the raw, probabilistic, entangled quantum universe and render it as a stable world of separate, persistent 'things' so we could navigate it. This biological collapsing of the wave function into useful certainties allows us to perceive a classical world of solid, separate objects. However, moments of diffuse attention allow for a temporary suspension of this filter, revealing the underlying unity of reality.

extra_fields.insight: The shift from seeing a blurred, unified field to distinct, separate objects mirrors the structure of objective reality as described by quantum physics. This suggests that our everyday perception is a constant act of collapsing reality into the familiar classical world.
